papers.title: First Human Cellular Reprogramming Trial Cleared by the FDA
papers.summary: Life Biosciences has received FDA clearance to begin the first-ever human trial of cellular reprogramming, targeting two age-related vision diseases. This is a landmark moment for the field — partial reprogramming moving from mouse studies into actual human patients for the first time. The trial tests whether Yamanaka factor-based approaches can reverse cellular aging in living human tissue.

papers.title: Icahn Mount Sinai Launches First Combination Anti-Aging Trial: Rapamycin + Lamivudine + Spermidine + Exercise
papers.summary: The first multi-intervention aging trial has launched at Icahn Mount Sinai, combining HIIT, resistance training, daily spermidine, and either rapamycin or lamivudine — an HIV drug that blocks LINE-1 retrotransposon reactivation, a key driver of inflammaging. Tracking 5,300 blood proteins simultaneously in 22 participants aged 65-80, this is the most sophisticated human aging trial ever run, and part of the $101M XPRIZE Healthspan competition. Single-drug aging trials are now officially obsolete.

papers.title: Cognitive Rejuvenation Through Partial Reprogramming of Engram Cells
papers.summary: A team published in Neuron has shown that applying partial reprogramming directly to memory-encoding neurons (engrams) rescues cognitive function in both Alzheimer's mouse models and normal aged mice. The approach restores youthful gene expression patterns in the specific cells responsible for memory storage, improving recall without triggering tumour formation. This is one of the most targeted demonstrations of reprogramming's potential in the brain to date.

papers.title: Clonal Haematopoiesis: The Different Flavours Linking Blood Mutations to Inflammaging
papers.summary: A comprehensive review in Aging Cell maps the various forms of clonal haematopoiesis (CH) — where blood stem cell mutations expand and dominate the immune system with age — and their overlapping connections to inflammaging, immunosenescence, and accelerated epigenetic aging. The key takeaway: CH doesn't just reflect aging, it likely accelerates it by feeding back into chronic inflammation. Different CH subtypes appear to synergise, compounding disease risk.

papers.title: Aged Gut Microbiome Drives Sepsis Severity via Histamine-Producing Klebsiella
papers.summary: Researchers have identified a specific mechanism by which the aged gut microbiome worsens outcomes in sepsis: elevated Klebsiella aerogenes in older hosts produces excess histamine, which suppresses Nlrp6 signalling and impairs autophagy in the gut lining, collapsing the intestinal barrier. Transplanting aged faecal microbiota into young germ-free mice reproduced the damage, while blocking the HA-Nlrp6-LC3 axis reversed it. This mechanistic clarity opens a new therapeutic target for infection in older adults.

papers.title: Senolytic-Resistant Senescent Cells Have a Distinct SASP — Senosensitizers Are the Next Step
papers.summary: Not all senescent cells die when treated with senolytics like dasatinib+quercetin — a subset survives, and this study in Aging Cell shows those resistant cells have a uniquely toxic secretome (SASP) that may be more damaging than regular senescent cells. The finding explains why senolytic trials show partial but incomplete benefits and points toward a new class of drugs called senosensitizers, designed to strip away senescent cells' resistance mechanisms before killing them.

papers.title: DNA Methylation Signatures of Cellular Senescence Are Not Reversed by Senolytic Treatment
papers.summary: A sobering finding in Aging Cell (March 2026): even after senolytic drugs successfully clear senescent cells, the epigenetic marks associated with senescence remain in the tissue. This means senolytics fix the inflammatory problem but leave behind a methylation 'scar' that epigenetic clocks will still detect as aged. Combining senolytics with partial reprogramming or methylation-resetting approaches may be necessary to fully reverse the biological age signature.

papers.title: Increasing Senolytic Effectiveness by Stressing Mitochondria
papers.summary: Researchers publishing in Nature Aging have uncovered that mitochondrial stress is a core mechanism behind how senolytics selectively kill senescent cells — these cells are uniquely vulnerable when their already-stressed mitochondria are pushed further. The insight opens a rational design pathway for next-generation senolytics that deliberately target mitochondrial dysfunction, potentially making senescent cell clearance far more efficient than current drugs like dasatinib+quercetin.
